selected_target.title: Topological Defects in Active Matter x Stem Cell Niche Architecture
selected_target.field_a: Active matter physics — topological defects in biological nematic systems
selected_target.field_c: Stem cell niche biology — spatial organization and mechanical regulation of adult stem cell niches
bridge_concepts: +1/2 comet defects as compression zones that concentrate morphogens
-1/2 trefoil defects as extrusion sites maintaining niche purity
YAP/TAZ mechanotransduction at defect stress sites maintaining stemness
Intestinal crypt positioning as topological defect lattice
Hair follicle spacing as epidermal nematic defect tiling
selected_target.why_selected: Highest confidence (8/10), strongest bridge mechanisms via YAP/TAZ, immediately testable with existing imaging technology, and DISJOINT literatures maximize novelty potential

literature_context: Active matter physics has established that topological defects (+1/2 comet, -1/2 trefoil) in cell monolayers concentrate mechanical stress and drive cell fate decisions (Saw 2017, Maroudas-Sacks 2021). Stem cell niche biology has established that mechanical forces regulate stemness via YAP/TAZ mechanotransduction, and that niche positioning is invariant after injury. The gap: nobody has mapped nematic alignment fields in adult tissues to determine whether stem cell niches correspond to topological defect positions. Key anomalies include unexplained crypt budding/fission patterns and the geometric precision of niche reformation after injury. No review articles connect active matter defects to stem cell niche positioning.
disjointness_status: DISJOINT — No cross-citations between active matter topological defect literature and stem cell niche positioning literature. Some work on mechanics in stem cells exists but does NOT use topological defect framework.

critic_questions.question: Can you address the developmental timing issue? Does nematic order exist in intestinal epithelium BEFORE crypt positions are established?
Can you identify specific morphogens with restricted diffusion (D ~ 1 um2/s) that are relevant to the proposed defect-concentration mechanism?
How do you reconcile the 2D defect splitting mechanism with the fundamentally 3D nature of crypt fission?
All surviving hypotheses rely on intestinal epithelium having measurable nematic order. Can you provide evidence or argument for why this tissue should be nematic?

quality_gate.title: Organoid Symmetry Breaking Is a Topological Defect Nucleation Event
quality_gate.reason: Mathematically rigorous topological argument with exceptionally testable predictions. 9/9 rubric points satisfied.

quality_gate.title: Activity-Dependent Crypt Fission Is Triggered When Local Epithelial Contractility Exceeds the Nematic Defect-Splitting Threshold
quality_gate.reason: Quantitative discriminating prediction (blebbistatin blocks fission despite Wnt activation). Physics framework solid, extrapolation reasonable.

quality_gate.title: Wound-Induced Topological Defects Serve as Transient Stem Cell Attractors That Become Permanent Niches When Pinned by ECM Stiffness Gradients
quality_gate.reason: Best-grounded hypothesis. Multiple independently grounded components. WIHN model provides elegant test. Clinical relevance via Marjolin ulcer.
